examples.title: Array Declaration and Access
examples.description: Demonstrates 1D array declaration, initialization, and element access in Python
examples.code:
# 1D Array in Python (using list)
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]

# Accessing elements by index
print(arr[0])  # Output: 10 (first element)
print(arr[4])  # Output: 50 (last element)
print(arr[-1]) # Output: 50 (negative indexing)

# Modifying element
arr[2] = 35
print(arr)  # Output: [10, 20, 35, 40, 50]

# Array length
print(len(arr))  # Output: 5
examples.explanation: Python lists are dynamic arrays. Index starts at 0. Negative indices access elements from the end (-1 is last element).
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for access and modification by index
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of elements

examples.title: 2D Array in C
examples.description: Demonstrates 2D array declaration and traversal in C using row-major order
examples.explanation: C uses row-major order by default. arr[i][j] accesses row i, column j. Memory is allocated contiguously row by row.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n×m) for n rows and m columns

examples.title: Linear Search Implementation
examples.description: Implements linear search to find an element in an unsorted array
examples.code:
def linear_search(arr, target):
    """
    Linear search algorithm
    Returns index if found, -1 otherwise
    Time: O(n), Space: O(1)
    """
    for i in range(len(arr)):
        if arr[i] == target:
            return i  # Element found at index i
    return -1  # Element not found

# Test the function
arr = [64, 34, 25, 12, 22, 11, 90]
result = linear_search(arr, 25)
print(f"Element found at index: {result}")  # Output: 2

result = linear_search(arr, 100)
print(f"Element found at index: {result}")  # Output: -1
examples.explanation: Linear search checks each element sequentially until target is found or array ends. Works on both sorted and unsorted arrays.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) worst case, O(1) best case (element at first position)

examples.title: Binary Search Implementation
examples.description: Implements binary search for a sorted array using divide and conquer approach
examples.code:
def binary_search(arr, target):
    """
    Binary search algorithm for sorted arrays
    Returns index if found, -1 otherwise
    Time: O(log n), Space: O(1)
    """
    left, right = 0, len(arr) - 1
    
    while left <= right:
        mid = (left + right) // 2  # Find middle index
        
        if arr[mid] == target:
            return mid  # Element found
        elif arr[mid] < target:
            left = mid + 1  # Search right half
        else:
            right = mid - 1  # Search left half
    
    return -1  # Element not found

# Test the function
sorted_arr = [11, 22, 33, 44, 55, 66, 77, 88, 99]
result = binary_search(sorted_arr, 55)
print(f"Element found at index: {result}")  # Output: 5

result = binary_search(sorted_arr, 100)
print(f"Element found at index: {result}")  # Output: -1
examples.explanation: Binary search repeatedly divides the search interval in half. Requires sorted array. Much faster than linear search for large arrays.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log n) - halves search space each iteration
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) iterative, O(log n) recursive for call stack
